coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of glutathione in bacteria and cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Lina
Xiao, Huangpei
Xue, Jiaxin
Lin, Xiaoyun
Zhu, Zhenmao
Liu, Ailin
Lin, Liqing
coredata.dc:description:
Background The sensitivity of 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) method may not be enough to detect low concentration glutathione (GSH), especially in complex samples, which limits its application in some biologic scenarios. Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant interest in bioanalytical applications due to their unique properties, particularly the sensitivity in the detection of complex samples.
Results In this work, carbon quantum dots (TOCDs) were prepared via pyrolysis method of d-threonine and o-phenylenediamine. Considering advantages of selectivity of DTNB and sensitivity of TOCDs, a simple, fast and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on TOCDs and DTNB was fabricated for selective detection of GSH. In the presence of GSH in the mixture of TOCDs and DTNB, –SH of GSH can interact with DTNB and subsequently affording 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoate (TNB), which can strongly absorb excitation light of TOCDs and lead to fluorescence quenching of TOCDs. As a consequence, quantitative screening of GSH can be achieved through the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs was decreased after the addition of GSH in the system of TOCDs and DTNB. Under optimized conditions, good linear relationships in the range of 2.5–40 nM, 40–150 nM and 150–1000 nM for GSH. TOCDs can effectively detect GSH with a limit of detection (LOD) 1.5408 nM. The feasibility of the TOCDs probe was verified through non-invasive detection of reduced glutathione (GSH) in bacteria and cells. The fluorescence of TOCDs in different living bacteria/cells were yellow fluorescence, while the fluorescence could be ignored after joining GSH and DTNB. The mechanism about quench phenomenon of the fluorescence intensity between TOCDs and DTNB/GSH was the inner-filter effect. These results demonstrated that the presented fluorescent sensor could be a promising sensing platform for GSH analysis.
Significance This sensing strategy could be expanded to more access to the GSH-induced acute toxicity and disease. The yellow fluorescence carbon quantum dots demonstrated its potential application on GSH detection in bacteria and cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor
Inner-filter effect
Glutathione detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bacterial and cells imaging of TOCDs
Detection of GSH in bacterial and cells samples
Detection of GSH by TOCDs in real samples
The inner-filter effect on quenching mechanism of TOCDs
Design strategy and the feasibility analysis of the detection of GSH by TOCDs
Strongly selective and strong excellent anti-interference of TOCDs
The bioimaging ability to bacteria and cells of TOCDs
Detection of GSH by TOCDs in bacteria and cells
Detection of GSH by TOCDs in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of TOCDs. (A) HR-TEM image of TOCDs. (B) The average hydrodynamic diameter and (C) the zeta potential of TOCDs. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of TOCDs. (E) Excitation-emission matrix spectra of TOCDs solution. (F) PL QY of TOCDs. The results obtained were expressed as average ± standard deviation from at least three repeats.
The surface group characterization of TOCDs. (A) The survey XPS spectrum of TOCDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) O1s.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of TOCDs, TOCDs + DTNB and TOCDs + GSH, respectively. (B) The feasibility analysis of the detection of TOCDs by the fluorescence spectra of TOCDs and different concentration of TOCDs + DTNB + GSH. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectra of DTNB decreased after the addition of GSH and the emission peak of TOCDs in DTNB and GSH solution at 560 nm. (D) TEM images of TOCDs, GSH and DTNB.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of TOCDs and TNB from DTNB + GSH (B) The relationship between the solution color and the increased concentration of GSH. (C) Fluorescence intensity of TOCDs + DTNB under different GSH concentrations (2.5–20,000 nM). (D) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of TOCDs + DTNB and GSH concentration (2.5–40 nM). (E) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of TOCDs + DTNB and GSH concentration (40–150 nM). (F) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of TOCDs + DTNB and GSH concentration (150–1000 nM). The data shown in the figures represent the average of three independent experiments (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The bioimaging ability to different bacteria of TOCDs and (B) the quenching phenomenon by the GSH and DTNB in bacteria, including S. aureus, E. coli, C. albicans, E. faecalis, S. epidermidis and S. marcescens.
Schematic illustration of TOCDs for the detection of GSH in bacteria and cells (by figdraw.com).
Detection of the QE at different concentration of GSH before and after background correction by the developed probe.
The fluorescence sensing of GSH using different method.
Detection of GSH in S. aureus by TOCDs in standard recovery experiment.
Detection of GSH in Hep G2 cell by TOCDs in standard recovery experiment.

Detection of the amounts of GSH through the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs in B16F1, Hep G2, RAW264.7, H9C2, S. aureus, E. coli, C. albicans, E. faecalis, S. epidermidis and S. marcescens, respectively.
Detection of GSH in serum by TOCDs in standard recovery experiment.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of glutathione in bacteria and cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Science, Huaqiao University, Quanzhou 362021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Shengli Clinical Medical College of Fujian Medical University, Fujian Provincial Hospital, Fuzhou University Affiliated Provincial Hospital, Fuzhou, 350001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Zhongshan Hospital (Xiamen), Fudan University, Xiamen, 361000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anxi Third Hospital, Quanzhou, 362400, Fujian Province, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anxi Third Hospital, Quanzhou, 362400, Fujian Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sensitivity of 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) method may not be enough to detect low concentration glutathione (GSH), especially in complex samples, which limits its application in some biologic scenarios. Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant interest in bioanalytical applications due to their unique properties, particularly the sensitivity in the detection of complex samples.

In this work, carbon quantum dots (TOCDs) were prepared via pyrolysis method of d-threonine and o-phenylenediamine. Considering advantages of selectivity of DTNB and sensitivity of TOCDs, a simple, fast and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on TOCDs and DTNB was fabricated for selective detection of GSH. In the presence of GSH in the mixture of TOCDs and DTNB, –SH of GSH can interact with DTNB and subsequently affording 5-thio-2-nitrobenzoate (TNB), which can strongly absorb excitation light of TOCDs and lead to fluorescence quenching of TOCDs. As a consequence, quantitative screening of GSH can be achieved through the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs was decreased after the addition of GSH in the system of TOCDs and DTNB. Under optimized conditions, good linear relationships in the range of 2.5–40 nM, 40–150 nM and 150–1000 nM for GSH. TOCDs can effectively detect GSH with a limit of detection (LOD) 1.5408 nM. The feasibility of the TOCDs probe was verified through non-invasive detection of reduced glutathione (GSH) in bacteria and cells. The fluorescence of TOCDs in different living bacteria/cells were yellow fluorescence, while the fluorescence could be ignored after joining GSH and DTNB. The mechanism about quench phenomenon of the fluorescence intensity between TOCDs and DTNB/GSH was the inner-filter effect. These results demonstrated that the presented fluorescent sensor could be a promising sensing platform for GSH analysis.

This sensing strategy could be expanded to more access to the GSH-induced acute toxicity and disease. The yellow fluorescence carbon quantum dots demonstrated its potential application on GSH detection in bacteria and cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of TOCDs for the detection of GSH in bacteria and cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) can effectively control the cells growth, which is important regulatory substance in maintaining the normal immune function. However, the excessive express of glutathione may destroy the health of human beings, especially the respiratory tract and renal system [1]. Under normal conditions in cells and tissues, more than 90 % of GSH is reduced, and the rest is oxidized [2]. When the cells and tissues change to pathological state, the little oxidized GSH will no longer remain. Obviously, GSH is an important indicator for the early detection of the human health, which maintains the repair of biomacromolecule [3]. Some diseases, including infection, cancer, aging and some other ailments maybe resulted from the abnormal levels of GSH [4]. Therefore, detection of GSH levels is of great importance for human health. Nevertheless, the detection for studying the demand and functional impact of GSH in bacteria and cancer cells have not been widely studied.

In bacteria, low molecular weight mercaptan, especially GSH can enhance the health and survival of bacteria under harsh conditions, such as anti-oxidative stress and regulation of pathogenesis [5]. GSH plays diverse roles in virulence, including activating virulence gene expression and promoting the formation of optimal biofilm. Additionally, GSH can also be converted into hydrogen sulfide, which is vital for the pathogenesis of some bacteria [6]. GSH and other low molecular weight mercaptans are produced in some bacteria, which affect the virulence of bacteria. It is necessary to build a visual sensing tactics for GSH in bacteria. In cancer cells, GSH is often present at elevated concentrations. This overabundance of GSH endows cancer cells with enhanced antioxidant capabilities, which contributes to their survival, proliferation, and resistance to chemotherapy and radiotherapy [7]. Therefore, the accurate and sensitive detection of GSH in both bacteria and cancer cells holds great promise for understanding biological processes, diagnosing diseases, and evaluating treatment efficacy.

Up to now, the GSH sense tactics with high sensitivity and accuracy mainly include the electrochemical method, surface enhanced raman scattering, different chromatography, mass spectrometry and the combinations of above [8,9]. However, the wide application of the above tactics is restricted by their complexity, cost, and time associated. Electrochemical methods and mass spectrometry, despite their high sensitivity, require complex sample processing, are prone to interference and require expensive instruments with high-level operation skills. With so many shortcomings to overcome, simple and portable GSH sensing tactics include colorimetric analysis, fluorescence analysis, immunochromatography, electrochemical analysis and chemiluminescence have been developed [10]. Recently, various biosensors based on fluorescence emitting carbon quantum dots have attracted widespread attention [11]. Fluorescent carbon quantum dots are nano optical functional materials that differ in structure and size from molecular luminescent materials, which have similarities in photophysical and photochemical properties with the former. Different materials-modified fluorescent sensor using different carbon sources-based carbon quantum dots for the detection of metal ions [12], drugs [13], nitrite [14], dopamine [15] and GSH [16]. Therefore, multifunctional carbon quantum dots should be developed, which can visually trace bacteria or cells and sense the existence of GSH in bacteria and cells.

Herein, a carbon quantum dots derived from o-phenylenediamine and d-threonine (d-threonine and o-phenylenediamine based carbon quantum dots, TOCDs) with yellow fluorescence light was developed and served as a probe for GSH detection in bacteria and cells. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs was strong in the system of TOCDs and DTNB. However, the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs was quenched after the addition of GSH in the system of TOCDs and TNB. In the reaction system, TOCDs can effectively detect GSH with a limit of detection (LOD) 1.5408 nM. Due to the good water solubility and biocompatibility, the TOCDs were applied to bio-imaging of GSH in bacteria and cells with outstanding yellow fluorescence. There were negligible fluorescence intensity after adding the GSH and DTNB in bacteria and cells, which meant the excellent sensing efficiency of TOCDs to GSH in bacteria and cells. Furthermore, this sensing strategy could potentially be extended to further investigate the GSH-induced acute toxicity and infectious disease.

d-threonine (D-Thr) and o-phenylenediamine were all provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 5,5′-Dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) and trinitrobenzene (TNB) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The glutathione (GSH) was purchased from Shanghai Biyuntian Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All other reagents and chemicals were of at least analytical reagent grade. Solutions were prepared with water purified by a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, USA).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was collected using a NICOLET iS50 Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA) transmission electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Particle diameter and Zeta-postential were tested and obtained from Litesizer 500 Nanoparticle Size and Zeta Potential Analyzer (Anton Paar, Austria).

The fluorescent TOCDs were prepared via a pyrolysis strategy according to a previous report with some modifications [16]. Briefly, TOCDs were synthesized by solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine and d-threonine. First, 10 mL deionized water, 10 mL o-phenylenediamine (0.2 g/mL) and 10 mL d-threonine solution (0.2 g/mL) were mixed, and the mixture was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon lining. Then the mixture was reacted for 24 h at 180 °C. After the reaction cooled naturally, it was filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. To eliminate extra impurities, the resultant solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min. After filtration and centrifugation, the supernatant was dialyzed (MW = 100–500 Da) in water for 48 h. Finally, the product was lyophilized to obtain the final products.

Typically, equal volumes of TOCDs dispersion (0.5 mg/mL) and DTNB solution (5 × 10−6 M) were mixed as the working solution for the detection of GSH. TOCDs solution (0.5 mg/mL), GSH solution (50 μL, 0.3–20000 nM) and PBS (1 mL, 0.1 M, pH 7.0) were mixed evenly and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. After incubation, the fluorescence emission spectra of the resulting mixture were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The slits of the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 10 nm wide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial and cells imaging of TOCDs
ce:section.ce:para: S. aureus, E. coli, C. albicans, E. faecalis, S. epidermidis and S. marcescens were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) nutrient broth at 37 °C for 24 h, respectively. The purified and re-suspended bacterial suspension (1.0 × 107 CFU) was incubated with TOCDs for 6 h in a shaking incubator (250 rpm) at 37 °C [17]. The bacteria were harvested by centrifuging the mixture at 3000 rpm/min for 10 min in Eppendorf (EP) tubes. The resulting bacteria were re-suspended and washed with sterilized water three times. Then 10 μL of the sample was dropped on a confocal Petri dish and dried at 37 °C to be observed in confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) with 30 % power of diode laser. All fluorescence images were captured by CLSM with a × 100 oil-immersion objective and taken under the same optical conditions. The same brightness and contrast were applied to the images by the microscope automatically.

B16F1, Hep G2, RAW264.7 and H9C2 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), respectively. Different cells were seeded in a 35 mm plate and cell lines were maintained at 37 °C for 24 h in a humidified incubator containing 5 % CO2, respectively. After the removal of culture medium, the cells were incubated with fresh culture medium containing 100 μL of aqueous solution of TOCDs for 6 h. After that, the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) by three times and cell imaging was carried out on fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in bacterial and cells samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the feasibility of the probe, residues of GSH in bacteria and cells were detected. The accuracy of the detection method was ascertained of the spiked recovery assays. The preparation of enhanced samples was as follows: S. aureus and Hep G2 (2 mL) was spiked with known concentrations of GSH standard solutions, respectively. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra were analyzed after adding GSH and DTNB to the S. aureus and Hep G2 solution, respectively. The samples were then left for 1 h at room temperature and stored overnight in the refrigerator to allow GSH to be fully absorbed. To monitor the absorption of GSH, different samples were incubated with of GSH at 10 nM. For bacterial samples, different bacteria were grown to the mid-exponential phase. The concentrations of GSH at 10 nM were added to separate aliquots of the bacterial cultures at 1.0 × 107 CFU/mL. After the 1 h room-temperature incubation, the bacteria were separated from the supernatant by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was carefully collected and the bacterial pellet was washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to remove all the unabsorbed GSH. The GSH concentration of the resuspended bacterial pellet in PBS was measured. For cell samples, different cells were seeded in 24-well plates and allowed to adhere overnight. The concentrations of GSH at 10 nM were added to the cell culture medium 1.0 × 104 cells/mL. After the 1 h room-temperature incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove all the unabsorbed GSH. The constructed probe was used to detect the samples, which was pretreated before detection. Briefly, the mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred to another centrifuge tube, and the residue was added with 2 mL water for repeated separation. The supernatant was mixed and filtered via a 0.45 μm filter. Thereafter, the fluorescence emission was then measured at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH by TOCDs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For serum samples, we collected human serum samples from healthy donors. To prepare the samples for GSH detection, we first diluted the serum 1:10 with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to reduce potential interference from high-molecular-weight proteins and other components. For urine samples, we collected human urine samples and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to remove any sediment. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to further eliminate potential particulate matter. Similar to the serum experiments, the urine samples were spiked with GSH at 10 nM. Regarding environmental water samples, tap water were choosed to evaluate the response to GSH of TOCDs.

TOCDs derived from o-phenylenediamine and d-threonine were applied to bio-imaging of GSH in different bacteria. This paper aims to demonstrate a fluorescence probe for the detection of GSH through the fluorescence quenching effect of TOCDs by TNB from the reaction of GSH and DTNB.

The carbon quantum dots were synthesized from o-phenylenediamine and d-threonine (d-threonine and o-phenylenediamine based carbon quantum dots, TOCDs). The TOCDs was further optimized by many factors, including the ratio of raw materials, reaction temperature, reaction time and so on. The conditional optimization results showed when the mass ratio of raw materials (o-phenylenediamine and d-threonine) was 1:1, the temperature of reaction was 180 °C and the reaction time was 12 h, the fluorescence performance of TOCDs was the best (Fig. S1). The follow-up research was carried out with TOCDs under the above condition.

The characterization of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that the TOCDs had good stability and dispersibility. The particle diameter of TOCDs was 2.63 ± 0.36 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 A. The average hydrodynamic diameter and the zeta potential of TOCDs were 7.14 ± 0.39 nm (Fig. 1B) and +21.34 ± 0.26 mV (Fig. 1C), respectively. These results demonstrated the TOCDs in good monodispersion with high crystallinity were successfully prepared. The stability of TOCDs in solution is an important parameter. The fluorescence intensity of TOCDs showed negligible variation at different stored conditions and stabilized within 20 days (Fig. S2). In details, TOCDs was stored under three different storage conditions including refrigeration at 4 °C (Fig. S2A), room temperature (about 25 °C, Fig. S2B) and in a 37 °C incubator (Fig. S2C). The fluorescence intensity was regularly detected on the 1st, 2nd, 5th, 8th and 20th days. After statistical analysis, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the experimental results under different storage conditions was less than 5 %, which strongly demonstrates that TOCDs have good stability under different storage conditions.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of TOCDs has characteristics related to its structure. As shown in Fig. 1D, TOCDs demonstrated a distinct absorption peak at 420 nm, which might be π-π ∗ transition caused by π electron energy level on the surface of TOCDs. Fig. 1E presented that the max emission peak of the TOCDs was 560 nm as the wavelength of excitation at 420 nm. Meanwhile, TOCDs showed strong yellow photoluminescence under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (inset in Fig. 1E). The corresponding emission spectrum under different excitation wavelengths changed from 340 nm to 500 nm of TOCDs embodied that the peaks of emission spectrum were nearly not shifted, indicating TOCDs had excellent monodispersion (Fig. S3A). The fluorescence quantum yield was determined by relative method. According to the method reported in the literature [18], 6 G of rhodamine (R6G) dissolved in absolute ethanol was used as the standard. As shown in Fig. 1F, the relative fluorescence quantum yield rate of TOCDs was measured and computed to be 19.23 % at 420 nm excitation. The compared relative fluorescence quantum yield of R6G was 94 % at 480 nm excitation. The stability of the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs was measured under excitation wavelengths at 420 nm every 5 min in Fig. S3B, which showed robust fluorescence stability of excitation light resistance.

The whole x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed the surface chemical properties of TOCDs. TOCDs was composed of C, N and O (Fig. 2 A). The high-resolution XPS peaks of C 1s at 284.98 eV and 286.08 eV manifested the existence –CO/C–N and C–C/CC, respectively [19] (Fig. 2B). The peaks of N 1s at 399.62 eV and 401.08 eV manifested the existence of –NH2 and C–N–C [20] (Fig. 2C). The peaks of O 1s at 532.38 eV and 532.72 eV of TOCDs could be divided into two peaks, belonging to –CO and C–O [21], respectively (Fig. 2D). The molecular structure and chemical composition of TOCDs were characterized through the FTIR spectra (Fig. S4). The absorption peaks at 1689.34 cm−1 and 3414.78 cm−1 appeared in o-phenylenediamine and TOCDs was phenolic hydroxyl groups and –NH2 groups, respectively. The absorption peak of TOCDs at 700-1400 cm−1 was attributed to the C–C and CC bond stretching vibration. The above data demonstrated surface chemical groups of TOCDs include -C-N, C–C/CC, -C-O, –CO, phenolic hydroxyl groups and –NH2 groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inner-filter effect on quenching mechanism of TOCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mechanism of the presented sensor were shown in Scheme 1 and Fig. S5. The fluorescence quenching of fluorescent materials can result in different quenching mechanisms, including the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [22], the internal filtration effect (IFE) [23], the dynamic quenching effect (DQE) [24] and the static quenching effect (SQE) [25]. The quenching mechanism of the TNB from DTNB and GSH on TOCDs was investigate by studying the above effects experimentally. Fig. S5A showed excitation peak at 420 nm and fluorescent emission peak at 560 nm of TOCDs, and a strong and broad absorption peak at 412 nm of TNB. Considering that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TNB and the fluorescence emission spectrum of TOCDs had great overlapping, which might be caused by the FRET. However, except the fluorescence spectrum of TOCDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of quencher, the fluorescence lifetime of TOCDs must be shortened in the FRET [26]. As shown in Fig. S5B, the fluorescence lifetime of TOCDs was 2.78 ns. The fluorescence lifetime of TOCDs was 2.68 ns with no obvious lifetime decay for after reacting with TNB from DTNB and GSH, indicating that static quenching of the TOCDs in turn-off process. The results showed that the fluorescence lifetime was basically unchanged after TOCDs interacted with quencher TNB from DTNB and GSH, which showed that the quenching mechanism was not FRET.

Generally, fluorescence can also be quenched by DQE and SQE. DQE refers to the collision between TOCDs and the quencher, which causes TOCDs to return from the excited state to the ground state [27]. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of TOCDs will be altered. Fig. S5B showed that the fluorescence lifetime of TOCDs remained essentially unchanged before and after the interaction with DTNB + GSH, indicating that the quenching mechanism was not DQE. The following three points need to be satisfied to demonstrate that the quenching mechanism of TOCDs by DTNB + GSH was SQE. Firstly, when carbon quantum dots interact with the quencher to form a non-fluorescent ground-state complex, SQE occurs. Secondly, the fluorescence lifetime of carbon quantum dots remains constant. Thirdly, the absorption spectrum of carbon quantum dots changes [28,29]. TOCDs had an ultraviolet absorption peak at 420 nm. After adding DTNB + GSH, the ultraviolet absorption peak of TOCDs did not change, indicating that no non-fluorescent ground-state complex was formed. Therefore, there are no DQE and SQE between TOCDs and DTNB + GSH.

Then, the possibility of TNB from DTNB and GSH quenching TOCDs through the IFE was further investigated according to the verification method of referring to literature reports [30]. The fluorescence quenching efficiency after background correction was calculated through combining with the geometry of the fluorescent cuvette used in this experiment (Fig. S5C). As shown in Fig. S5D and Table 1 , with the increase of GSH concentration, the fluorescence quenching efficiency measured at each concentration was significantly different from that after background correction. The results showed that TNB from DTNB and GSH had a significant IFE on the quenching process of TOCDs.
In conclusion, IFE was considered as the main fluorescence quenching mechanism of the TOCDs due to GSH/DTNB in this system by experimental studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design strategy and the feasibility analysis of the detection of GSH by TOCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the paper, TOCDs was served as a fluorescence probe for the detection of GSH through fluorescence quenching effect. The fluorescence spectra of TOCDs in Fig. 3 A showed no difference without or with DTNB and GSH, respectively. TOCDs showed strong and yellow fluorescence in absence of GSH, suggesting scare influence of GSH on fluorescence of TOCDs. Similarly, a slight fluorescence quench for TOCDs could be observed with presence of DTNB, which maybe resulted from a weak decomposition of DTNB into TNB. Upon addition of GSH into the mixture of TOCDs and DTNB, reaction between –SH of GSH and DTNB occurred subsequently yielding a yellow colored TNB. TNB display a strong absorption at 412 nm and can absorb excitation light at 420 nm, leading to a significant fluorescence quenching. Therefore, qualitative and quantitative analysis for GSH can be achieved based on the fluorescence changes. As showed in Fig. 3B, after the different concentration of GSH was added, the disulfide bond in DTNB breaked into TNB molecule, which would quench the fluorescence of TOCDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3C displayed the absorption peak of DTNB decreased after the addition of GSH, indicating that DTNB had been decomposed to TNB. TEM images in Fig. 3D exhibited that the TOCDs lost its uniform dispersed state after the introduction of the GSH and DTNB. The above results were the evidence that the TNB, the redox reaction product between GSH and DTNB, could quench the fluorescence of TOCDs. These demonstrated that the sensor system as-fabricated would be effective for GSH detection.

Because the fluorescence of TOCDs can be quenched by the TNB from GSH and DTNB reaction product. Furthermore, different concentrations of the GSH were incubated with DTNB and TOCDs to evaluate the fluorescence quench ability of TOCDs for GSH under the best detection conditions. The fluorescence of the stylet dispalyed the best detection property to GSH (6 × 10−6 M) when the concentration of DTNB was 5 × 10−6 M, the reaction time between GSH and DTNB was 30 min, the response time between GSH, DTNB and TOCDs was 30 min, the pH of buffer solution was 7.4, and the concentration of TOCDs was 0.5 mg/mL. As showed in Fig. 4 A, the TOCDs showed obvious fluorescence intensity under the emission at 420 nm but the TNB from DTNB and GSH had no excitation fluorescence. Under the optimum conditions, with the concentration of GSH increased, the fluorescence intensity at 560 nm visibly reduced and the hue of yellow liquor deepened, which gradually changed from light yellow to deep yellow under visible light (Fig. 4B). It was obvious that the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs decreased when the concentration of GSH increased, which showed that there was a negative correlation between TOCDs and DTNB + GSH (Fig. 4C). The fluorescence intensity was negatively linearly correlated with the concentration of GSH changing from 2.5 nM to 40 nM (Fig. 4D), 40–150 nM (Fig. 4E) and 150–1000 nM (Fig. 4F), respectively. F = −2.87 [GSH] + 863.93 (R2 = 0.98599), F = −0.52 [GSH] + 658.87 (R2 = 0.99734), F = −1.37 [GSH] + 805.51 (R2 = 0.99781) were expressed as the regression equation of TOCDs to GSH, respectively. The limit of detection was 1.5408 nM, which calculated according to the fixed formula of limit of detection (the slope of the standard curve was divided by 3 times the standard deviation of the blank). Meanwhile, the reproducibility of the detection of GSH (10 nM) by TOCDs for 15 times was good, as showed in Fig. S6. Regarding the experimental repeatability, the reproducibility of the detection of GSH (10 nM) by TOCDs for 15 times was good under the same experimental conditions (the pH of buffer solution was 7.4, the temperature was 25 °C), as showed in Fig. S6A. As presented in Fig. S6B, the reproducibility of the detection of GSH (10 nM) by TOCDs for 15 times was good under the different experimental conditions. These findings support the attained fluorescence intensity decrease investigation, the repeatability of the experiments and the long-term stability of TOCDs with DTNB and GSH. Moreover, a comparison of our work with other similar methods recently reported for GSH detection were shown in Table 2 . Compared from three aspects, it was observed that this work it can give a fast test time and a better detection limit, cost-effectiveness and the potential for in-situ and real-time detection for GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strongly selective and strong excellent anti-interference of TOCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Although the method of fluorescent detection of GSH using TOCDs is attractive, how to distinguish GSH from other biothiols remains a challenge, as they possess similar structures and reactivity. Meanwhile, strongly selective and excellent anti-interference of TOCDs in the detection of GSH are the key analytical indicator. There were several potential interfering biothiols, amino acid and metal ion were selected as representative for inspection of TOCDs, including GSH, cysteine, homocysteine, coenzyme A, lipoic acid, d-arginine, d-lysine, d-tyrosine, d-aspartic acid, d-alanine, d-methionine, d-phenylalanine, d-leucine, d-threonine, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, I−, Br−, Cl−, SO4 2−, CO3 2− and PO4 3−. Only GSH could produce obvious changes in the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs under the DTNB solution. However, the fluorescence intensity of TOCDs in other interferingmetal ion (Fig. S7A) and amino acid (Fig. S7B) solutions remained almost unchanged, which showed the highly selective and strong anti-interference of TOCDs for GSH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The bioimaging ability to bacteria and cells of TOCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To be an excellent candidate for bioimaging applications, TOCDs should exert minimum interference on bacterial. We thus explored the viability of TOCDs towards to different bacterial, including S. aureus, E. coli, C. albicans, E. faecalis, S. epidermidis and S. marcescens. According to the results in Fig. S8, the vitality of TOCDs with bacteria could not be affected, which suggested its potential bioimaging to bacteria. As shown in Fig. 5 , the imaging results indicated the six kinds of bacteria were stained with TOCDs to a certain degree, which suggested that TOCDs stained bacteria. After the incubation of TOCDs with bacteria for 6 h, the fluorescence microscopic image showed strong yellow fluorescence signal of TOCDs, as shown in Fig. 5A. From Fig. 5B, it was evident that the yellow fluorescence significantly decreased in bacteria when the TOCDs was incubated with DTNB and GSH in bacteria. Meanwhile, the viability of TOCDs towards to different cells, including B16F1, Hep G2, RAW264.7 and H9C2 cells were explored. According to the results in Fig. S9, the vitality of TOCDs with different cells could not be affected, which suggested its potential bioimaging to different cells. As shown in Fig. S10, the imaging results indicated the four kinds of cells were stained with TOCDs to a certain degree, which suggested that TOCDs could stain cells. After the incubation of TOCDs with cells for 6 h, the fluorescence microscopic image showed strong yellow fluorescence signal of TOCDs (Fig. S10A). It was evident that the yellow fluorescence significantly decreased in cells when the TOCDs was incubated with DTNB and GSH (Fig. S10B). The bioimaging ability to bacteria and cells of TOCDs facilitated its application as a biocompatible bioimaging probe in bacteria and cells. There is great potential in integrating TOCDs-based imaging with therapeutic strategies.

For in-vivo toxicity assessments, we utilized the mouse model to conduct in-vivo toxicity evaluations. Body weight was measured daily for a period of 7 days (Fig. S11A), which showed no abnormal in body weight among the experiment and control groups. Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining was performed on the tissue sections, which revealed no obvious pathological changes in the organs of the TOCDs-treated groups compared to the control group (Fig. S11B). There were no signs of inflammation, necrosis, or abnormal cell morphology in the liver, kidney, heart, spleen, and lung tissues. These results preliminary indicated that the TOCDs did not produce toxicity in vivo and was safety in living organisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH by TOCDs in bacteria and cells
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections is one of the major global health concerns. For bacterial infections, TOCDs can detect infection in response to local GSH-related signals. S. aureus was taken as representative bacteria to confirm the practical application potential of TOCDs for GSH detection. Whether the analysis method of TOCDs for GSH detection was suitable for S. aureus should be investigated by the spiked recovery assay. As exhibited in Table 3 , the recoveries of GSH (10 nM) in S. aureus was 105.64 %, which was between 80 % and 120 %. The relative standard deviations (RSD) were lower than 1.06 %. The result suggested the analysis way of TOCDs for GSH detection are suitable for S. aureus, which has broad application prospect.

The redox imbalance characteristic of malignant transformation is obvious in early cancer diagnosis, particularly the elevated GSH levels. This early detection can provide crucial time for intervention and improve patient prognosis. In cancer therapy, TOCDs can image the tumor microenvironment based on GSH levels. Hep G2 cell was taken as representative cancer cells to confirm the practical application potential of TOCDs for GSH detection. Whether the analysis method of TOCDs for GSH detection was suitable for Hep G2 cells should be investigated by the spiked recovery assay. As exhibited in Table 4 , the recoveries of GSH (10 nM) in Hep G2 cells was 105.99 %. The relative standard deviations (RSD) were lower than 2.20 %. The result suggested the analysis way of TOCDs for GSH detection are suitable in cancer cells, which has broad application prospect.

The GSH concentration in different bacteria (S. aureus, E. coli, C. albicans, E. faecalis, S. epidermidis and S. marcescens) at 1.0 × 109 CFU/mL and cells (B16F1, Hep G2, RAW264.7 and H9C2 cells) 1.0 × 106 cells/mL were calculated through the linear relation of fluorescence intensity and the concentration of GSH, respectively (F = −2.87 [GSH] + 863.94 (F:750–900), F = −0.52 [GSH] + 658.87 (F:600–750), F = −1.37 [GSH] + 805.51 (F:150–600), Table 5 ). The results showed that the concentration of GSH in different bacteria were different but negligible. Especially, the concentration of GSH in cancer cells and normal cells were different obvious, which could be used to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. Therefore, the TOCDs fluorescence probe could detect the level of GSH in bacteria and cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH by TOCDs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: By utilizing TOCDs to detect GSH in clinical samples such as tissue biopsies or body fluids, we can potentially identify early-stage cancer or infectious diseases. Meanwhile, the detection ability of TOCDs in complex biological samples and environmental samples are needed to evaluate its feasibility and anti-interference ability in practical applications. To confirm the application potential of TOCDs in the detection of GSH in real samples, we took serum, urine and tap water as representative sample and used the spiked recovery assay to study the applicability of TOCDs for GSH detection. As exhibited in Table 6 , the recovery of GSH (10 nM) in serum, urine and tap water were 108.42 %, 102.45 % and 107.39 %, respectively (falling within the range of 80 %–120 %). The relative standard deviations (RSD) were lower than 1.19 %, 4.02 % and 1.83 %, respectively. The results demonstrated that our sensor could accurately detect GSH in the complex biological samples and environmental samples.

To recap, a fast and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots was effectively fabricated to the sensitive detection of GSH based on IFE between TOCDs and DTNB. TNB generated from GSH and DTNB could easily quench the fluorescence of TOCDs as the result of IFE. The concentration of GSH was measured by monitoring the fluorescence intensity changes of TOCDs. TOCDs exhibited highly selective and strong anti-interference to GSH, which was responsive to the discernible fluorescence intensity. There are robust fluorescence stability and storage stability of TOCDs. More importantly, the fluorescent sensor has been successfully used to quantify GSH in bacteria and cells. In the system of the detection of GSH in bacteria or cells, the analysis method of TOCDs is suitable in the spiked recovery assay, respectively. The fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots provide its potential application prospects in diagnosis and evaluation of the level of GSH in bacteria and cells.

Lina Wu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Huangpei Xiao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiaxin Xue: Writing – review & editing. Xiaoyun Lin: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Zhenmao Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ailin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Liqing Lin: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82273981), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2022Y0009), the Startup Fund for Scientific Research of Fujian Medical University (2021QH1296) and the Scientific Research Funds of Huaqiao University (Grant No. 22BS129).
